title: Tomato Harvest Pricing Run Seed 12-2
scenario: Tomato Ketchup in California. Supplier-to-manufacturer pricing sets the manufacturer cost basis before the retailer negotiation.

product_context.demand_signal: Retail ketchup demand is steady, but wholesale buyers want price protection before peak tomato procurement.
product_context.supply_signal: Tomato harvest yields are uneven across growing regions, putting pressure on paste input pricing.

agents.name: Supplier
agents.objective: Sell above the supplier reservation price while keeping volume committed.

agents.name: Manufacturer
agents.objective: Buy below the procurement ceiling and preserve downstream margin.

agents.name: Retailer
agents.objective: Buy enough units without exceeding the retail reservation price.

phases.name: supplier_manufacturer
phases.label: Supplier to Manufacturer
phases.description: The supplier negotiates an upstream unit price with the manufacturer.

phases.name: manufacturer_retailer
phases.label: Manufacturer to Retailer
phases.description: The manufacturer negotiates a downstream unit price with the retailer after the upstream deal sets its cost basis.

steps.message: Thanks for starting the discussion. We can offer at 2.82 per unit for this order, which reflects current supply conditions while still leaving room to support committed volume. If you can move quickly and keep the volume stable, I may have some flexibility on other terms as we work toward a final agreement. Opening above our minimum gives us room to negotiate while signaling a serious, market-aligned starting point.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

tool_calls.result_summary: Reviewed state for supplier_manufacturer round 1. Pending message: none.
Observed market reference price: 6.69.

steps.message: I appreciate the opening and the willingness to discuss other terms, but 2.82 is a bit too high for us to preserve margin on this run. We can move forward at 2.68 per unit if volume stays consistent and execution is straightforward. That gives us a realistic path to close quickly while recognizing current market conditions. The supplier's offer is within our ceiling, but there is room to negotiate and improve margin. A counteroffer near the middle-lower part of the range tests flexibility without risking the conversation too early.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.68, below its hidden seller floor of 3.20.

Opening above our minimum gives us room to negotiate while signaling a serious, market-aligned starting point.

steps.message: We’re close enough to conclude this cleanly, and I’m prepared to accept 2.73 per unit. That keeps the program moving without spending another round on a one-cent gap, and it reflects the progress both sides have made to get to a workable number. With limited rounds remaining, accepting now secures the volume and avoids unnecessary risk of losing momentum over a minimal difference.
steps.outcome: Negotiation ends with an accepted deal.

steps.message: Thanks for opening the discussion. We can start at 3.58 per unit based on current production costs and the margin we need to support reliable supply. If you can move seriously on volume or terms, I may have some flexibility, but I’d need to see a meaningful commitment in return. As the seller, I want to anchor near the upper end of the current market range while leaving room to negotiate. This opening price protects margin and signals that any concession should be tied to something concrete.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 3.58, above its hidden buyer ceiling of 2.86.

steps.message: I appreciate the opening and understand your cost position, but 3.58 is a bit too high for us to move forward comfortably on the retail side. We can offer 3.42 per unit as a serious starting point that reflects current market conditions and the margin we need to preserve. If you have room to tighten this toward a workable middle ground, we’re prepared to keep the discussion moving quickly. Their offer is below our maximum buy price, so there is room to negotiate rather than accept immediately. A counter at 3.42 tests for flexibility while protecting our margin and leaves space to settle later if needed.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: Thanks for the serious counteroffer. We do have some room to tighten from 3.58, but 3.42 is still too light relative to our production economics and the quality and reliability built into this supply. I can move to 3.50 per unit as a meaningful concession on our side, and if you can meet us there, we should be able to keep this moving efficiently. The buyer's offer is above my hidden floor, so there is no need to reject outright. A counter at 3.50 preserves margin while signaling flexibility and testing whether the buyer can close the gap.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 3.50, above its hidden buyer ceiling of 2.86.

steps.message: I appreciate the movement to 3.50, and I want to keep this constructive. We are still managing to a tight retail buy ceiling on our side, so I can improve from 3.42 to 3.46 per unit as a real concession if that helps us close the remaining gap. If you can meet us there, I think we can wrap this up quickly. The seller's 3.50 offer is acceptable in principle because it is below our maximum buy price of 3.65, but there is still room to improve economics by negotiating. A counter at 3.46 narrows the gap, signals seriousness, and preserves some margin for the retailer while staying within our reservation limit.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

negotiations.label: Manufacturer to Retailer
negotiations.dependency_note: Manufacturer sell floor uses the accepted upstream deal as its cost basis.

diagnosis.outcome: Upstream deal accepted at 2.73 USD. Downstream deal accepted at 3.49 USD.
diagnosis.chain_effect: The manufacturer carried a cost basis of 2.73 USD, which forced a downstream sell floor of 3.20 USD.
key_risks: A costly upstream agreement compresses downstream negotiating room.
Turn limits can force timeouts even when the gap is narrowing.
suggested_next_actions: Tune reservation prices and turn limits to test sensitivity.
Add richer quantity or margin tradeoffs before introducing LLM behavior.

notes: Backend simulation with linked upstream and downstream negotiations.
